event: Scenes from the Passion of Christ
symbols: cross — the instrument of salvation, the paradox of divine glory revealed through shame and suffering
winding city streets — the Via Dolorosa, the path of suffering that leads from condemnation to Golgotha
crowds — humanity in its complicity, indifference, and compassion before the suffering Christ
architectural cityscape — the new Jerusalem rendered as a Flemish city, making the Passion present to the contemporary viewer
multiple scenes in one panel — the medieval practice of continuous narrative, inviting meditative progression through each station of suffering
description: A densely packed panel depicting multiple episodes of Christ's Passion within a single continuous cityscape. The composition weaves through elaborate medieval and Flemish-style architecture -- towers, gabled houses, archways, and city gates. In the foreground and middle ground, numerous small figures enact various Passion scenes: Christ before Pilate, the flagellation, the carrying of the cross through winding streets, and other moments. Crowds fill every space. The upper portions show distant buildings and a pale sky.
theological_description: Hans Memling's simultaneous Passion narrative is a masterwork of late-medieval devotional art that invites the viewer into an extended meditation on Christ's suffering. The continuous narrative format -- presenting multiple episodes within a single unified cityscape -- mirrors the practice of the Stations of the Cross and the late-medieval emphasis on compassio, the believer's empathetic participation in Christ's pain. Each scene corresponds to Gospel accounts: the trial before Pilate (Matthew 27:11-26), the scourging (John 19:1), the carrying of the cross (Luke 23:26), and other Passion events. The theological weight of the Passion rests on the doctrine of vicarious atonement: Christ suffers willingly to bear the sins of the world (Isaiah 53:4-6), and his journey from condemnation to crucifixion enacts the priestly self-offering described in Hebrews 9:11-14. The architectural density of the scene, with its winding paths and multiple viewpoints, embodies the complexity of salvation history converging on one week in Jerusalem. Memling's choice to set these events in a familiar Flemish cityscape follows the Devotio Moderna tradition: the Passion is not a distant historical event but a present reality demanding the viewer's response.

scripture_refs.reason: Christ's trial before Pontius Pilate, one of the key scenes depicted in the continuous narrative.
The scourging and mocking of Christ with the crown of thorns, visible among the depicted episodes.
The way to Golgotha, Simon of Cyrene compelled to carry the cross, the mourning women following.
The Suffering Servant who bore our griefs and carried our sorrows -- the prophetic foundation of the entire Passion.
Christ as the high priest who enters the holy places through his own blood, securing eternal redemption.
Mark's account of the flogging, the mocking by soldiers, and the beginning of the road to crucifixion.
Christ's self-humiliation, becoming obedient unto death, even death on a cross.
